speaker: interviewer
text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewer
text: Second tape with Aldean Mason.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aldean, you said you were-- you had heard the stories of the, the camp, what was going on. Had you ever heard anything prior to going into Buchenwald about any concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. I never heard anything prior to what was going on in, in the camps at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jewish people that were traveling in your unit with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, there were. The number-- I'm not sure whether there was 15 to 20 Jewish people, yes. And I'm-- I know [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for a fact-- I know of one report of a Jewish-- one of the Jewish fellows that was in the camp, or that was in our unit and at Buchenwald concentration camp and that had no idea at all of any of this. And the Jewish members of our unit-- and I'm sure there were more than one, but I can speak for one. But I'm sure the ones that were Jewish were very helpful to the liberated prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they would write letters for them and to their relatives or-- in the United States. And some of them could speak Yiddish? Would-- Yiddish, mm-hmm and, and talk to them. And they were very kind, and very compassionate to these liberated ones that they found in Buchenwald. When you went into the camps, you said you saw the bodies. Did you see any of the living prisoners that were coming through or walking around?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't talk to them. But they were-- there were living ones walking around, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would your description be of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of them? I think most of them were like skin and bone-- just skin hanging on their body. They were very thin, and paper thin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you observe about the camp when you looked around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't go exactly-- I didn't go right into the camp. I just saw the buildings, and saw these prisoners that were walking around. And, of course, our eyes were on the trucks load of-- the bodies that were loaded there that they had-- that this was the day that these town people, German town people, and mayor of the city came in. And the diplomats, or senators, from the United States were there on that same day to observe them, to go through the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody describe what the camp was like inside? Did they tell you what was going on there, as far as the crematoriums and gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the liberated prisoners mentioned this to the unit do-- to our doctors and to our enlisted men, the ones that were in there to take care of them, and how many slept on the tiers of, of their bunks. And they found that-- in the crematoriums, they found remains of the bodies there. And it was something to clean up. And then, of course, I think some of them had a little advance notice. They-- the commandants and some of those got out of there-- knew that the army was coming on, the infantry men or the army, that the camp was going to be liberated. But they found atrocious messes there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of the nurses at all go in to the camp itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure of that. Maybe the chief nurse did, an assistant. I'm not-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in any discussions with the other soldiers who were in there and were coming out? Did they share any information with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only later what-- you know, what I read. You mean the soldiers that were in there? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of any German soldiers still there? Did anybody mention them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened, then, after you left Buchenwald? Where did you-- proceed to what you set up then.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Buchenwald, then, like I told you, we were sent, I believe, to the 120th Evacuation Hospital for detached service. Then we received orders and traveled on to Kamm, Germany. And our enlisted men then were released from their duty at B-- at Buchenwald. And they joined us there. And we went into this village of Kamm, Germany. And that was when we really-- we were setup, I believe, in four hospitals. And we had prisoners, or the patients from all around the camps that-- where they had been liberated. And we really for the first time in that hospital were working as a very active hospital taking care of all these sick people that were coming in. And they were even doing surgery. The doctors that were surgeons had some surgery to do. And they were busy with intravenous. And it was very busy there because I thought-- I don't know at one time either we had 1,240 patients there, and then setup in all-- in those four hospitals. But we were just-- we were busy taking care of, of all these liberated patients.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned four hospitals. How were these hospitals set up? What was taken over to make them hospitals?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was four hotels. So they had to get cots in and-- in the rooms and set them up for them, for the patients. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they had to have the mess operation. And they set up a pharmacy. And they set up the surgery. And they were busy. Those that couldn't eat, they had IVs going, and blood plasma going. And, and they were busy all the time. I believe that we did-- I know the enlisted men did, I think, 12 hour duty. And the nurses, I'm not sure whether they did 12 or just eight hours that they went in. There were just-- there were 40 nurses, including the captain and the assistant nurse, so probably 38 nurses, or 37. And then we also had like a-- the nurses also had-- we had a psychiatric doctor. So they were-- maybe had ward there, and has-- worked with some patients on, on a ward or that might have had some problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were all your personnel United States Army? Or did you use any town people in the hospitals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, we did. We had some for-- they were hired for different tasks. I think they were hired in the kitchen, or to help, and washing. And then the Red Cross nurses were in that area, too, that would take patients to and fro, and those that were transferred. And it was just a busy time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember if any of the town's people got to see actual pis-- prisoners that were being brought in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sure they did. The town was small, and I thought that it had swelled to about 10,000 afterwards on that-- with all the people coming in. It was a small village. So, yes, they would see the prisoners coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember if they ever reacted to what they saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. It was-- I don't remember. I think that they were hospitable, hospitable to us, the townspeople. Well, we-- I don't know how much because we really came in and took over their town. We were in a square. And the nurses, for instance, moved into this apartment building, or it could have been a home-- three story. And imagine taking over their bedroom and taking-- coming into their living room. And they would have their steins around and everything. And so they came every day to check to see what was around. So that's how we took over that little village and the square. And I'm sure it was the same with the enlisted men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel that they hampered you in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- no, but I, I could understand now why they were concerned of-- that we moved into some of their areas. And not all their-- they couldn't take all their furniture out. And, as I say, they had their mementos too. But I sure-- I can remember a few of them coming and walking through the rooms, and seeing if everything was in the order that they left them. That's just my memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much information were you given about the prisoner-- the concentration prisoners that were brought to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- I wasn't given any information, only from the ones that maybe spoke English if you talked to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any patients that spoke English?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had several. And when I say several, we had a few. Mm-hmm. And then also in our units, several of the men could maybe speak German, and another language that helped out. But I don't remember information, especially that they gave me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you care for these people in their very frail condition? What did you do for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We would give them-- bathe them. And enlisted men did this too. And see that they were fed. And see that that their linens, or that their clothing, was clean. That was a big-- the task of their washing and it was a big task of taking care of blankets and, and their clothes, and just do the best that we could to make them well, and to make them feel that they knew that they were being cared for. They were very gracious for anything that you did. You could tell it in their eyes. And they knew that the Americans were there to help them. And I think all in our unit really did their very best to help them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find them able to talk when-- or did you find them so melancholy, or depressed that they wouldn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure that some of them were. And like I say, only those that maybe spoke a little English that you really knew what was going on, other than to look at them in their-- at their face and see expressions with their eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would they hold on to you, touch you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they would hold your hand. And you might just fluff up their pillow, and move their head. And then some of them, as they felt better, they walked around. And, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were taken care of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides the malnutrition, what other diseases would they come in with, or problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had-- there was some typhus, and many with pneumonia, many with pneumonia. And see there, again, you'd have to take care of their linen, and if they ran high temperature. And they had diarrhea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel you had enough medication to help you through these times?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think they had a-- I don't think that they had all the medication they needed. I think they had to get their supplies from other areas. I think they had a truck going out for different supplies at times because there was many in the beginning to take care of.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were nursing the prisoners, what were you hearing about the rest of the war front?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that these camps were being liberated. And we felt that, that soon that news would come that the war would be ended there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any prisoners, hospital patients now, of course, who left and kept in contact with you, or wrote to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever receive a letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, I never-- oh, I received a letter in Kamm, Germany, yes, I did, from a Mr. Jack Pinto thanking the US Army for saving his life. And, and I carried that with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it. Mm-hmm. I've had it 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you read it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. I'd be happy to read it. US Army Hospital, Passau, Germany, 16th of May, 1945. I thank the US Army for saving my life. I've been three years in the concentration camp and was very ill. The fifth of May, as angels sent from heaven, US soldiers came in the camp. And 130 boys, more dead than alive, were saved. The US Army boys are working day and night to get us strong again. May God bless the US Army with freedom, and that they will soon be home by their family. These-- this are my wishes, Jack Pinto.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you received that letter, what did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt that he was really special. And I could understand-- I could understand his feelings for the Americans coming in and saving them. And he, I'm sure, really appr-- he had to be-- have very deep feelings to write this-- write these note-- write this note. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I had-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, did you give yourself the credit for saving lives?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't-- no. Nuh-uh. I didn't give myself credit, no. I just-- I gave all of the enlisted men, and the doctors, and everyone-- the Red Cross help, and all. I just-- it was-- I didn't feel it was-- I didn't save that life. I maybe helped. But I didn't-- no, all of them combined, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all of the units that comprised-- not just the Army. But there are other units too that were in there in the-- over in Europe. And I guess I was just felt I was a little part of it, a small part of, of being able to, to be an Army nurse, and to have been over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides Mr. Pinto's letter and his feelings, did you ever relate to any other special patient of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: No special stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: No special, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the other nurses? Would they share any special stories of other patients of theirs at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know of any right now. I'd be interested to hear of-- I haven't-- I'm sure that maybe there are some special stories of somewheres with patients. I've had many special stories with my patients, but not in Europe where I worked in a station hospital, and taking care of patients, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You indicated earlier that you thought you were going to treat all the thousands of wounded American soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever feeling less than what you wanted to do, or angry that you didn't get to serve that part of the service?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh I th-- excuse me. I think I would have loved to have gone to-- on to the Pacific. And this is where we thought we were going. And we had big signs written on our-- I guess with chalk or something-- on our trains, and saying that we were C-- what is it? CBI bound, or the Pacific. And-- but then when the war-- the war-- well, the war ended in Europe. But we went home for a leave. And we went home for the leave to be ready to go to the Pacific.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in July of 1945. And then when the war ended in August of 1945 in the Pacific, then that changed many plans. And no one-- they were eager to go on. I mean, we had been in Europe, and we were ready to go on. Then they started the point system of being discharged from the service. And so then you took a second look. And you could have stayed in the Army. And there would have been many fields that you could have gone on to. But I chose being honorably discharged from the Army at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was end of the year, November of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you resume your life after you were discharged?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I was discharged, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I worked as a nurse in a-- in the Snohomish County health department in the state of Washington. And then later on, I returned-- or I moved to San Diego, California and worked as a staff nurse there. And I loved it very much. And then later on, the former Sergeant Mason came into my life--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that. --from the 120th Evacuation unit, and that kind of changed my plans. And then we were married in 1949. And then I moved out to the middle west. How did Sergeant Mason get in contact with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wrote me a little note, or sent me a little card, and sent it to an old address. And I wasn't there. But the ca-- my sister forwarded the card to me. And so when I saw it, I was really surprised. And then I just wrote him a note. And so that's how it started. I couldn't believe it. And there was a possibility that I would never receive that note, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you and Sergeant Mason have many discussions, or get together at all during your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he return-- visited in San Diego in 1948. And he seemed pretty nice, and he was through college. And so we were married in '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get to know each other during your unit together at the 120th Evacuation Hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no just in speaking, and saying-- just talking, hello, and really never had any chats over five minutes, if we lingered into, to knowing him. I used to carry the drug basket down to the pharmacy, and just say, hello, Sergeant. And that was it. And so, no, we didn't have any secret place that we met or anything like that. And he was just a nice sergeant. So we really met-- or got to know him afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: You shared a lot of experiences together going into Buchenwald, being near there, and in the hospital setup. Did you ever talk about that later on in your marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many times. Yes, it would come up. And we were able to-- it was-- it's real helpful. Many things that he didn't know, and many things I didn't know of what occurred in the area-- yes, we have, many times. And, of course, I would say since we started this search-- I didn't start it. A Ruth Dixon from Spokane, Washington, Ruthie Dixon should be given much credit in organizing. She was the coordinator of starting a-- this unit to get together for our 50th year anniversary. No one had seen each other. And then we-- she thought it was only going to be a little thing, maybe 25 people. And maybe that we'd find out. And through the years, we were after the war, busy with our life, everyone was, getting back from the war. And so they lost acquaintance with buddies that they had met, enlisted men had met. And so we really worked on it. And then we had our first reunion in 50 years last September, 1994 in Kansas City, Missouri. And it was one of the happiest times of our life, really happy. And they were wonderful men. And they've had wonderful lives. And we're still-- we're going to have a reunion this fall. And we still want to find out what's happened to the 38 people-- want to locate them if we can to just know the whereabouts. And many of them are in their late 70s and 80s. And we had a doctor that came in September last year, Dr. Major-- well, Dr. Anthony Andriola. He was from New York. And he-- and living in Clearwater, Florida. And he was just a jewel. And everyone was so happy to see him. And he was looking forward to coming to Newport. And he passed away this past April, but so there'll be new faces to see this year.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many did attend your reunion?

speaker: interviewee
text: 89, with the spouses.
